methods.name: Vertical thermal-identity shear
methods.hypothesis: A sloping frontal/baroclinic zone should show large thermal-identity change between neighboring pressure levels as well as horizontal identity contrast at the level being plotted.
steps: Match raw temperature to same-longitude climatology and compute thermal-displacement score at every 250-1000 hPa level.
For each level, compare that score with the nearest pressure level above and below.
Score cells where vertical thermal-identity shear and horizontal thermal-identity gradient are both high.
Pick the strongest midlatitude ridge by longitude in each hemisphere.

methods.name: Warm/cold advection dipole
methods.hypothesis: Hot/cold fighting should be active where winds are advecting tropical-like identity into nearby polar-like identity, or vice versa.
steps: Compute thermal-displacement score and its horizontal gradient.
Read ERA5 wind at the same pressure level and time.
Compute thermal-identity advection, then smooth nearby warm-advection and cold-advection lobes.
Score cells where opposite-signed advection lobes touch near a thermal-identity gradient.

methods.name: Theta-e gradient with displacement contrast
methods.hypothesis: Air-mass fights often sharpen equivalent potential temperature, so a theta-e gradient becomes more relevant when it also separates tropical-like and polar-like thermal displacement.
steps: Compute equivalent potential temperature from temperature and specific humidity.
Smooth theta-e about 500 km and compute its horizontal gradient.
Sample thermal-displacement score across that theta-e gradient direction.
Score cells where theta-e changes sharply and the warm/moist side is more tropical-like than the cold/dry side.

methods.name: Thermal-wind baroclinicity
methods.hypothesis: A real baroclinic boundary should align with vertical wind shear because horizontal temperature contrasts imply thermal-wind shear.
steps: Read wind at every 250-1000 hPa level.
Compute vertical wind-shear magnitude against neighboring pressure levels.
Multiply shear by the local thermal-displacement horizontal gradient.
Pick the strongest shear-supported thermal-identity ridge by longitude.

methods.name: Raw-isotherm-normal displacement jump
methods.hypothesis: Use real smoothed-temperature isotherms for geometry, then ask whether crossing those isotherms also crosses a polar-like to tropical-like displacement jump.
steps: Smooth raw temperature about 500 km and compute its gradient direction.
Sample thermal-displacement score on the warm and cold sides normal to the smoothed isotherms.
Score cells where real isotherms are packed and thermal-displacement score jumps across the isotherm normal.
Pick the strongest real-isotherm/displacement-jump ridge by longitude.

pruned_ideas.reason: Already tested in the previous pressure-level loop; it is too close to a normal-for-latitude crossing.
Already the strongest simple prior candidate, so this pass avoids repeating it as a standalone method.
Already tested and found useful as corridor width/confidence rather than line placement.
Already tested as process evidence; this pass uses different wind-process ideas instead.
Prior notes show they remove longitude-specific context and introduce hard-to-defend pressure jumps.
At fixed pressure levels it nearly duplicates raw-temperature displacement for this lookup.
Meteorologically real, but prior diagnostics show it is too generic and terrain/polar biased unless gated.
Useful as a contour proposal, but it does not directly measure spatial hot/cold opposition.
